trace.rationale: Historical data shows that in MLB games, the first inning has a run scored approximately 55% of the time, based on general patterns from pre-cutoff seasons. No specific team or pitcher adjustments are available, so the base rate is used.

Historical data shows roughly 50-55% chance of a run scoring in the first inning in MLB games, with slight variation based on team strengths. No specific team or pitcher info provided, so baseline estimate.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 52% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific team or pitcher info to adjust.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 48-52% of first innings have at least one run, with slight variation by team and pitcher. No specific game context provided, so baseline probability near 0.5.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 52% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific team or pitcher information to adjust from that baseline.
Historical MLB data shows runs are scored in the first inning roughly 45-50% of games, but slight edge to 'yes' due to typical scoring probability.
Historical base rate for runs in the first inning across MLB games is approximately 55%, based on league-wide data from recent seasons.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55-60% of first innings have at least one run scored, but without specific team/lineup info, I use the lower end of that range.

Historical MLB data shows roughly 50-55% of first innings have at least one run scored, with slight variation based on team strengths and starting pitchers. Without specific game details, the baseline probability is near 0.5, slightly tilted to Yes due to the overall scoring frequency.
